title: Bayon: the temple of 200 faces - Bruno Maestrini
highlights: temple. It was the official state temple of the king and was built in the 12th or 13th century. The building's trademark are the hundreds of faces found on several towers. Nobody knows exactly how many there were originally. It is estimated that there were about 50 towers, added at various points in time, and today only 37 survive. The Bayon has around 216 faces thought by some scholars to be the image of king Jayavarman VII himself, while others believe them to be the bodhisattva Lokesvara. Angkor Thom was the last capital of the Khmer empire before it's fall. It is the largest in area in today's Angkor  ... title: Bayon Temple: History, Architecture & Cultural Significance - Ourbuddhismworld.com
highlights: Bayon Temple, located in the center of Angkor Thom in Cambodia, is one of the most iconic monuments of the  ...    reflecting the king’s devotion to Mahayana Buddhism. The temple is best known for its massive stone faces carved into towers, each with a serene expression that evokes peace, compassion, and mindfulness. Today, Bayon stands  ...    Perhaps the most striking feature of Bayon is its towers, each adorned with four giant faces looking in the four cardinal directions. It is estimated that over 200 faces were originally carved across more than 50 towers. The faces have a gentle smile, closed eyes, and a sense of peaceful authority. They are widely believed to represent Avalokiteshvara, the bodhisattva of compassion, who is central to Mahayana Buddhism. Some scholars also argue that the faces resemble King Jayavarman VII, blending the identity of the king with divine compassion. This connection suggests  ... Since the Bayon’s rediscovery by French scholars and explorers in the 19th century, the identity of the smiling figures that greet visitors to the temple and to the city of Angkor Thom has been debated. Do they depict Buddhist or Hindu deities, or do they depict King Jayavarman VII smiling over his empire?
